text: Preserve read, update, and delete compatibility through the existing --request-id option.
goal:
Implement request handle adoption in APIEase CLI.

Background

APIEase requests now use three identifiers:

- id is server-owned, opaque, immutable, and still supported by existing APIs.
- handle is source-owned, stable, unique per shop, and should be the normal repository identifier.
- name is display text only.

The APIEase public request item route accepts the existing requestId path slot as either an id or a handle. Do not introduce a separate requestHandle parameter.

Goal

Make create request naturally idempotent by handle while preserving id compatibility for read, update, and delete.

Create request behavior

For create request:

If handle exists in the local request JSON:

- Validate that it uses the APIEase handle format.
- Look up the remote request with GET /api/v1/resources/requests/{handle}.
- If found, update it.
- If not found, create it.
- If the lookup fails for any reason other than not found, fail and do not create.

If handle is missing:

- Fail.
- Recommend rerunning with:
  --auto-update-source-identifier

If id exists and --auto-update-source-identifier is not set:

- Fail before remote lookup or create.
- Explain that request ids are server-owned.
- Recommend rerunning with:
  --auto-update-source-identifier

Read, update, and delete behavior

Read, update, and delete must continue accepting existing request ids.

The existing --request-id option remains for compatibility, but its value may be either a server-owned id or a source-owned handle because the APIEase public request route resolves id first and handle second.

Do not add a new --request-handle option in this task unless existing CLI conventions make aliases trivial and fully tested.

New option

Add:

--auto-update-source-identifier

This option may modify only identifier metadata inside the local request JSON.

It must never modify request configuration.

Never change:

- type
- method
- address
- parameters
- triggers
- liquid
- body
- nextRequest
- or any other functional configuration

Identifier migration rules

If handle already exists:

- Leave handle unchanged.
- Remove id if present.

Else if id exists:

- Determine whether id is UUID-shaped.
- UUID-shaped means 8-4-4-4-12 hexadecimal groups, case-insensitive.

If id is not UUID-shaped:

- Slugify id into handle using the APIEase handle format.
- Remove id.

Example:

Old source:

id = lookup-shopify-discount-code-admin-graphql

becomes:

handle = lookup-shopify-discount-code-admin-graphql

If id is UUID-shaped:

- Treat it as a leaked server identifier.
- Remove it.
- Derive handle from name.

Else:

- Derive handle from name.

Handle format

Generate handles as lowercase slug values using letters, numbers, and hyphens.

Use the same format as APIEase:

^[a-z0-9]+(?:-[a-z0-9]+)*$

If an existing handle is present but invalid, fail. Do not silently rewrite explicit handles.

If neither id nor name can produce a handle, fail with an error explaining that a handle cannot be inferred.

Create output

When create request creates a new remote request, report created.

When create request updates an existing remote request by handle, report updated.

JSON output should include enough structured information for automation to distinguish created versus updated.

Tests

Add tests for:

- create fails when id exists and auto-update-source-identifier is absent
- create fails when handle is missing and auto-update-source-identifier is absent
- auto-update-source-identifier moves non-UUID id to handle
- auto-update-source-identifier derives handle from name when id is UUID-shaped
- auto-update-source-identifier removes id when handle already exists
- auto-update-source-identifier derives handle from name when both id and handle are missing
- auto-update-source-identifier never changes functional request configuration
- create reads an existing remote request by handle and updates it
- create creates a new request when handle lookup returns not found
- create does not create when handle lookup fails for a transport, auth, server, or non-not-found error
- read request accepts the existing --request-id option with an id value
- read request accepts the existing --request-id option with a handle value
- update request accepts the existing --request-id option with an id value
- update request accepts the existing --request-id option with a handle value
- delete request accepts the existing --request-id option with an id value
- delete request accepts the existing --request-id option with a handle value
- CLI validation allows handle and rejects invalid handle
- CLI validation rejects source id for create unless auto-update-source-identifier is set
- human-readable output distinguishes created from updated
- JSON output distinguishes created from updated

Documentation

Update CLI documentation and examples to use handle instead of id for request source files.

Update command documentation to explain that --request-id remains the compatibility option name, but request read, update, and delete may now pass either an id or a handle.

Update template request examples if this task owns the template change. If template changes are handled in a separate repository task, call that out clearly in the completion summary.
context: The option value may be either a server-owned id or a source-owned handle.
steps: Review read request command handling to ensure --request-id values are passed through unchanged.
Review update request command handling to ensure --request-id values are passed through unchanged.
Review delete request command handling to ensure --request-id values are passed through unchanged.
Adjust any validation that incorrectly rejects handle-shaped values for --request-id.
constraints: Do not add --request-handle unless existing CLI conventions make aliases trivial and fully tested.
Do not break existing server-owned id usage.
acceptanceCriteria: Read request accepts --request-id with a server-owned id value.
Read request accepts --request-id with a handle value.
Update request accepts --request-id with a server-owned id value.
Update request accepts --request-id with a handle value.
Delete request accepts --request-id with a server-owned id value.
Delete request accepts --request-id with a handle value.
